skill.name: hosted-agents-v2-py
skill.description: Guides creation of Azure AI Foundry hosted agents with Python, Azure AI Projects SDK, custom container images, and managed identity authentication.
skill.summary: Build Azure AI Foundry hosted agents from trusted container images using the Python Azure AI Projects SDK.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many patterns, but review found they are primarily Markdown examples, SDK imports, placeholder URLs, and documentation links. The skill is not malicious, but it deserves a medium warning because it teaches custom hosted containers, external MCP endpoints, and environment variable propagation.

medium_findings.title: Custom Container Hosted Agent Deployment
medium_findings.description: The examples create Azure hosted agents from container images and enable agent tools. This is expected behavior, but users must trust images and restrict Azure permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code examples clearly create hosted agents from container images. This is a legitimate purpose, but the created workloads can execute custom code in Azure.

medium_findings.title: External MCP Server Configuration
medium_findings.description: The skill shows hosted agents configured with MCP server URLs. This is legitimate, but external tool endpoints can expand network and data exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly include MCP server URL fields. The URLs are placeholders, so the concern is operational exposure rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Blocks Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are Markdown examples, including a pip install command and Python snippets. They are not executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is inside documentation code fences and examples. No executable script file or runtime command wrapper is present in the analyzed file.

low_findings.title: SDK Imports Misclassified as Dynamic Imports
low_findings.description: The import findings are static Python import statements for Azure SDK model classes. They do not dynamically load attacker-controlled modules.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines show normal Python import syntax from documented Azure SDK namespaces. I did not find evidence of dynamic import expressions or obfuscation.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Reads Are Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The skill reads AZURE_AI_PROJECT_ENDPOINT from the environment and passes configuration to containers. No secret exfiltration or credential harvesting is shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only observed environment variable is an Azure project endpoint. The file also advises against hardcoding secrets, reducing concern.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Placeholders or Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The URL findings include an Azure endpoint placeholder, example MCP endpoints, and public documentation links. They do not indicate hidden callbacks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains are placeholders or official package and documentation references. I did not find a fixed collection endpoint for user data.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Not Supported by Context
low_findings.description: The cited lines are the description and Markdown table headers, not cryptographic code. No hashing, encryption, or weak algorithm usage appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain prose and table formatting. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Not Supported by Context
low_findings.description: The cited line describes a protocol version error and solution. It does not collect host, user, process, or network information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a troubleshooting table entry for Azure agent protocols. No reconnaissance command or system inventory logic is present.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner combined code examples, network placeholders, and environment reads into a critical heuristic. Review found no credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or malicious intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The ingredients are present only as documented Azure usage patterns. I did not find a hidden network destination, secret access beyond configuration, or executable payload.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hosted Agent Creation from Container Images
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples deploy container-backed agents. Use trusted images, pin production tags, and apply least-privilege Azure roles.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit in the hosted agent examples. It is a legitimate capability with meaningful operational risk if misused.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Tool Endpoint Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples configure MCP servers by URL. Users should only connect to trusted MCP servers with clear data handling controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The MCP URL fields are visible in the documentation. The endpoints are placeholders, so risk depends on user-selected servers.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Variables Passed to Agent Containers
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples pass environment variables into containers. Avoid passing secrets directly and prefer managed identity or Azure Key Vault.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples include environment variable propagation. The observed values are not secrets, but the pattern can expose sensitive configuration.

content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable guidance for creating container-based agents in Azure AI Foundry. This skill provides focused Python patterns for hosted agent versions, tools, and deployment settings.
actual_capabilities: Shows required Azure AI Projects SDK installation for hosted agent support.
Demonstrates DefaultAzureCredential setup with an Azure AI project endpoint.
Explains ImageBasedHostedAgentDefinition parameters for image, CPU, memory, and protocols.
Provides synchronous and asynchronous Python creation patterns for hosted agents.
Shows tool configuration for code interpreter, file search, and MCP endpoints.
Lists common hosted agent deployment errors and likely fixes.
limitations: It does not build or scan container images.
It does not create Azure resources outside the shown SDK calls.
It does not validate account capability host settings automatically.
It depends on beta Azure SDK behavior that may change.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Hosted Agent
use_cases.description: Create a first Python hosted agent version using a container image and the RESPONSES protocol.

use_cases.title: Prepare Agent Deployment Guidance
use_cases.description: Document required endpoint, managed identity, ACR pull permission, and capability host prerequisites.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Agent Version Failures
use_cases.description: Map common image, protocol, and capability host errors to practical remediation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Hosted Agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a basic Azure AI hosted agent in Python using ImageBasedHostedAgentDefinition and DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Add Resource Settings
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to configure CPU, memory, protocol versions, and environment variables for a hosted agent container.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Agent Tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add code interpreter, file search, and a trusted MCP server to an Azure hosted agent definition.

prompt_templates.title: Review Deployment Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my hosted agent plan for ACR permissions, capability host setup, image tagging, environment variables, and cleanup steps.

output_examples.input: I need a hosted agent that runs a container image from Azure Container Registry.
output_examples.output: A concise setup plan covering SDK version, Azure credential setup, project endpoint, container image reference, protocol version, and agent creation flow.

output_examples.input: My hosted agent fails to start after deployment.
output: Check ACR pull permissions for the project managed identity.
Verify the image path and tag exist.
Confirm the capability host is configured.
Use the RESPONSES protocol with version v1.

output_examples.input: I want to add tools to my hosted agent.
output_examples.output: Guidance for selecting code interpreter, file search, and trusted MCP endpoints while limiting unnecessary tool access.

best_practices: Use managed identity and least-privilege Azure roles for container image pulls.
Pin production container images to versioned tags instead of latest.
Keep secrets out of direct environment variables and use managed secret services.
anti_patterns: Do not deploy untrusted container images into hosted agent environments.
Do not connect hosted agents to unknown MCP servers.
Do not leave unused hosted agent versions running after tests finish.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you create Azure AI Foundry hosted agents that run custom container images through the Python SDK.

faq.question: Does it create the container image?
faq.answer: No. It assumes you already built and pushed a container image to a registry such as Azure Container Registry.

faq.question: Which authentication pattern does it recommend?
faq.answer: It recommends DefaultAzureCredential with the Azure AI project endpoint stored in an environment variable.

faq.question: Can it configure hosted agent tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows patterns for code interpreter, file search, and MCP tool configuration.

faq.question: What permissions should I check first?
faq.answer: Check that the project managed identity has AcrPull permission on the container registry.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium warning. The content is documentation, but it covers custom containers and external tool endpoints.
